questions.question: In the Body Paragraph 2 - Freedom's Daughters box, write a topic sentence that identifies the main idea the author develops about Barbara Johns' actions.

responses.content:
This story “freedom walkers” is about a young woman of color named Claudette who refused to give up her seat for a white male in a bus. This act of bravery led to others doing the same thing and important people wanting to change the worlds way of thinking. I know this because the story says. 
“During the next few years, other black riders were arrested and convicted for the same offense-sitting in seats reserved for whites. They paid their fines quietly and continued to ride the public buses. It took a spunky fifteen-year-old high school student to bring matters to a head.”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a topic sentence and one piece of evidence (the quote about other black riders being arrested and Claudette bringing matters to a head). However, it lacks a second piece of evidence and does not provide clear explanations for how the evidence supports the main idea. The response is on topic and shows understanding, but it is missing required elements for a fully correct answer.

responses.content:
This story “freedom walkers” is about a young woman of color named Claudette who refused to give up her seat for a white male in a bus. This act of bravery led to others doing the same thing and important people wanting to change the worlds way of thinking. I know this because the story says. 
“During the next few years, other black riders were arrested and convicted for the same offense-sitting in seats reserved for whites. They paid their fines quietly and continued to ride the public buses. It took a spunky fifteen-year-old high school student to bring matters to a head.” And “  In October 1955, several months after Claudette was convicted, Mary Louise Smith, an eighteen-year-old black girl, was arrested when she refused to move to the back of the bus so a white woman could take her seat. “[The driver] asked me to move three times,” Smith recalled. “And I refused. I told him, ‘I am not going to move out of my seat. I am not going to move anywhere. I got the privilege to sit here like anybody else does.’”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a topic sentence and two pieces of evidence from the text, but it does not provide explanations for how each piece of evidence supports the main idea. The response is missing the required explanations and a concluding sentence, which are necessary for a fully correct (3/3) answer. Therefore, it fits the 'partially-correct-high' category.

responses.content:
This story “freedom walkers” is about a young woman of color named Claudette who refused to give up her seat for a white male in a bus. This act of bravery led to others doing the same thing and important people wanting to change the worlds way of thinking. I know this because the story says. 
“During the next few years, other black riders were arrested and convicted for the same offense-sitting in seats reserved for whites. They paid their fines quietly and continued to ride the public buses. It took a spunky fifteen-year-old high school student to bring matters to a head.” And “  In October 1955, several months after Claudette was convicted, Mary Louise Smith, an eighteen-year-old black girl, was arrested when she refused to move to the back of the bus so a white woman could take her seat. “[The driver] asked me to move three times,” Smith recalled. “And I refused. I told him, ‘I am not going to move out of my seat. I am not going to move anywhere. I got the privilege to sit here like anybody else does.” This shows other black people who decided to follow her steps and protesting like their triend
 setter.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a topic sentence, two pieces of evidence from the text, and a brief explanation at the end. However, the explanation is not clearly connected to each piece of evidence separately, and the elaboration is not fully developed for both pieces. The concluding sentence is present but not clearly separated from the explanation. Therefore, it meets the criteria for 'partially-correct-high' but does not fulfill all requirements for 'correct' (3/3), which would require a clear explanation for each piece of evidence.

questions.question: In the same box, write the rest of your body paragraph, building on your topic sentence. Your paragraph should include at least two pieces of evidence, and an explanation of how each piece of evidence develops the main idea. Be sure to end your paragraph with a concluding sentence.

responses.content: This story is called “freedom’s daughters” and it is about a girl named Barbara who makes a students only assembly with help from other adults and her community and went on strike for a better school and equal rights. I know this because the. Story says “ She brought five other student leaders together for a clandestine student meeting in the bleachers of the school’s athletic field. Farmville’s black adults, she said, had made no headway in getting a new school. “Then,” recalled one of the other students at the meeting” in this meeting she talked about how they should do something about segregation, something that could change the world. I know this because the story says “ she said our parents ask us to follow them but in some instances—and I remember her saying this very vividly—a little child shall lead them. She said we could make a move that would broadcast Prince Edward County all over the world”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows understanding of the main events and includes some interpretation, such as Barbara Johns wanting to change segregation and lead others. However, the topic sentence is not clearly focused on the author's main idea about Barbara Johns' actions (such as her leadership, boldness, or challenging the status quo). The response also mixes summary and evidence, and the topic sentence is not a clear, concise claim. Therefore, it fits best in 'partial-understanding.'

responses.content: This story is called “freedom’s daughters” and it is about a girl named Barbara who makes a students only assembly with help from other adults and her community and went on strike for a better school and equal rights. I know this because the. Story says “ She brought five other student leaders together for a clandestine student meeting in the bleachers of the school’s athletic field. Farmville’s black adults, she said, had made no headway in getting a new school. “Then,” recalled one of the other students at the meeting” in this meeting she talked about how they should do something about segregation, something that could change the world. I know this because the story says “ she said our parents ask us to follow them but in some instances—and I remember her saying this very vividly—a little child shall lead them. She said we could make a move that would broadcast Prince Edward County all over the world” her act of bravery caused this “ The strike leaders sent a letter to the NAACP in Richmond that same afternoon. “We hate to impose as we are doing,” the letter began, “but under the circumstances we are facing, we have to ask for your help.” Three days later, two top civil rights lawyers were on their way to Farmville. By this time, the NAACP had already decided to challenge “separate but equal” in favor of total desegregation. Although there may have been, as Richard Kluger has written, “no less promising place in all Virginia to wage the fight for equal schools,” the NAACP lawyers were willing to try. In a shabby basement meeting room of a local church, they told the students and some parents that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community. The strikers were stunned but enthusiastic. “It seemed like reaching for the moon,” said Barbara Johns.’
responses.bucket_rationale: The response gives a detailed summary of events and includes some interpretation, such as calling Barbara's actions 'bravery' and mentioning her impact. However, the topic sentence is mostly a summary and does not clearly state the main idea or significance of Barbara Johns' actions in a single, focused claim. The response is also somewhat repetitive and lacks a concise analytical statement about what her actions reveal or why they matter. Therefore, it fits best in the 'partial-understanding' category.

responses.content: This story is called “freedom’s daughters” and it is about a girl named Barbara who makes a students only assembly with help from other adults and her community and went on strike for a better school and equal rights. I know this because the. Story says “ She brought five other student leaders together for a clandestine student meeting in the bleachers of the school’s athletic field. Farmville’s black adults, she said, had made no headway in getting a new school. “Then,” recalled one of the other students at the meeting” in this meeting she talked about how they should do something about segregation, something that could change the world. I know this because the story says “ she said our parents ask us to follow them but in some instances—and I remember her saying this very vividly—a little child shall lead them. She said we could make a move that would broadcast Prince Edward County all over the world” her act of bravery caused this “ The strike leaders sent a letter to the NAACP in Richmond that same afternoon. “We hate to impose as we are doing,” the letter began, “but under the circumstances we are facing, we have to ask for your help.” Three days later, two top civil rights lawyers were on their way to Farmville. By this time, the NAACP had already decided to challenge “separate but equal” in favor of total desegregation. Although there may have been, as Richard Kluger has written, “no less promising place in all Virginia to wage the fight for equal schools,” the NAACP lawyers were willing to try. In a shabby basement meeting room of a local church, they told the students and some parents that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community. The strikers were stunned but enthusiastic. “It seemed like reaching for the moon,” said Barbara Johns.” This hows that she is a brave and persistent personal who never stops until she gets what she wants.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response provides a clear topic sentence that interprets the significance of Barbara Johns' actions, stating she is "a brave and persistent person who never stops until she gets what she wants." This combines two related but different traits (brave and persistent), and the claim is supported by evidence from the text. The response is a complete sentence and aligns with the main idea about Barbara Johns' actions, not just listing events. There are no major quality issues, and the language is appropriate for a fourth-grade level.

questions.question: In the Conclusion box, write a concluding paragraph (3-4 sentences) that wraps up your comparison and explains why these stories matter.

responses.content: Here are a few parts of textual evidence that prove the last paragraph of my essay “ she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” The students cheered and marched out of the auditorium, with Barbara Johns in the vanguard.” and “ In the fall of 1950, Johns decided to take action on her own”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response provides two pieces of textual evidence from the excerpt about Barbara Johns, but it does not include a topic sentence, explanations for the evidence, or a concluding sentence. The response is not off-topic or incorrect, but it is missing key elements required for a complete body paragraph. Therefore, it fits best in the 'partially-correct-low' category.

responses.content:
Have you ever heard of people such as Rosa Parks and Martin Luther King Jr. people who protested for equal rights for colored people, or people who changed the world in their own way. Well here are some stories called “Freedom walkers” and “Freedom’s daughters” both texts show that young people like Claudette and Barbara played an important role in desegregation.This story “freedom walkers” is about a young woman of color named Claudette who refused to give up her seat for a white male in a bus. This act of bravery led to others doing the same thing and important people wanting to change the worlds way of thinking. I know this because the story says.
“During the next few years, other black riders were arrested and convicted for the same offense-sitting in seats reserved for whites. They paid their fines quietly and continued to ride the public buses. It took a spunky fifteen-year-old high school student to bring matters to a head.” And “ In October 1955, several months after Claudette was convicted, Mary Louise Smith, an eighteen-year-old black girl, was arrested when she refused to move to the back of the bus so a white woman could take her seat. “[The driver] asked me to move three times,” Smith recalled. “And I refused. I told him, ‘I am not going to move out of my seat. I am not going to move anywhere. I got the privilege to sit here like anybody else does.” This shows other black people who decided to follow her steps and protesting like their trend setter.This story is called “freedom’s daughters” and it is about a girl named Barbara who makes a students only assembly with help from other adults and her community and went on strike for a better school and equal rights. I know this because the. Story says “ She brought five other student leaders together for a clandestine student meeting in the bleachers of the school’s athletic field. Farmville’s black adults, she said, had made no headway in getting a new school. “Then,” recalled one of the other students at the meeting” in this meeting she talked about how they should do something about segregation, something that could change the world. I know this because the story says “ she said our parents ask us to follow them but in some instances—and I remember her saying this very vividly—a little child shall lead them. She said we could make a move that would broadcast Prince Edward County all over the world” her act of bravery caused this “ The strike leaders sent a letter to the NAACP in Richmond that same afternoon. “We hate to impose as we are doing,” the letter began, “but under the circumstances we are facing, we have to ask for your help.” Three days later, two top civil rights lawyers were on their way to Farmville. By this time, the NAACP had already decided to challenge “separate but equal” in favor of total desegregation. Although there may have been, as Richard Kluger has written, “no less promising place in all Virginia to wage the fight for equal schools,” the NAACP lawyers were willing to try. In a shabby basement meeting room of a local church, they told the students and some parents that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community. The strikers were stunned but enthusiastic. “It seemed like reaching for the moon,” said Barbara Johns.” This hows that she is a brave and persistent personal who never stops until she gets what she wants. Here are a few parts of textual evidence that prove the last paragraph of my essay “ she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” The students cheered and marched out of the auditorium, with Barbara Johns in the vanguard.” and “ In the fall of 1950, Johns decided to take action on her own” this proves her bravery and persistence, which got her a better school and made her a role model for many children all across the U.S. her taking action of her own is what caught important people’s eye and her protest did the same. These stories matter because they have an important role in desegregation. They also show role models and trend setters for children and people of all ages all over the country. These stories show the importance of standing up for what you believe is right and for what you want to change in the world.
responses.bucket_rationale: The paragraph contains several grammar and punctuation mistakes, such as missing commas, run-on sentences, and incorrect capitalization. These errors go beyond mere style or phrasing suggestions.

I told him, ‘I am not going to move out of my seat. I am not going to move anywhere. These stories matter because they have an important role in desegregation. They also show role models and trend setters for children and people of all ages all over the country. These stories show the importance of standing up for what you believe is right and for what you want to change in the world.
